prompt: Complete with the correct form: 'Il libro è ____.' (rosso)
explanation: Italian adjectives change ending to match the noun. Rosso is a Class I adjective with four forms -o/-a/-i/-e; for a m.sg noun the form is the dictionary one. Libro is m.sg, so rosso.

Most Italian adjectives sit after the noun they describe, in attributive position, and still have to agree with it. Nuovo is a Class I adjective; quaderno is m.sg, so the form is nuovo. Working: un quaderno nuovo.
When an adjective sits after 'to be', it's in predicative position but still has to agree with the subject. Italiano is a Class I adjective; Marco is m.sg, so the form is italiano.

prompt: Which is right? Complete: 'Il vino è ____.' (buono)
explanation: Buono shortens to buon before some masculine nouns (un buon libro), but only pre-noun. In predicative position, after 'to be', the Class I adjective keeps its full m.sg form. Il vino is m.sg, so buono.
examiner_note: Predicative buono. Pre-noun shortening (buon libro) is a separate bucket and is tested under special.buono.

explanation: Molto can be an adjective (and would then agree), but here it's an intensifier, like English 'very', so it doesn't change form. Agreement is with the subject. Alto is Class I; fratello is m.sg, so alto.
examiner_note: Light secondary touch on the invariability of adverbial molto; not bucketed, just noted.

explanation: Italian adjectives have to match in number as well as gender. Rosso is Class I, and the m.pl form ends in -i. Pomodori is m.pl, so the attributive adjective is rossi.
The classic miss with Italian adjectives is forgetting number agreement and leaving the singular. Rosso is Class I; with a m.pl subject in predicative position, the form is rossi. I libri sono rossi.
examiner_note: The diagnostic miss 'rosso' (no number agreement) is caught by must_not_include.

explanation: Italian adjectives agree with the noun they describe. Italiano is Class I; due ragazzi is m.pl, so the attributive form is italiani.
Italian adjectives match the noun in gender and number. Nero is Class I, with the four-form pattern -o/-a/-i/-e. Gatti is m.pl, so the predicative form is neri.

gender.reveal: masculine (m); invariable noun (il film, i film)

explanation: Some Italian nouns are invariable — film looks the same in singular and plural — but the determiner still tells you the number. Quei signals m.pl. Noioso is Class I, so the agreeing form is noiosi.
examiner_note: Tests that the learner agrees with the determiner/number, not the surface form of the noun.

explanation: Italian Class I adjectives have four forms — -o, -a, -i, -e — for the four gender-number combinations. With a feminine singular noun like mela, the form is -a. So la mela è rossa.
The diagnostic miss with feminine nouns is leaving the adjective in its m.sg citation form. Rosso is Class I; macchina is f.sg, so the predicative form is rossa.
examiner_note: Diagnostic miss 'rosso' (no agreement) caught by must_not_include.

explanation: Most Italian adjectives sit after the noun in attributive position and still have to agree. Rosso is Class I; una macchina is f.sg, so the form is rossa. Working: una macchina rossa.
Italian adjectives match the noun in gender and number. Alto is Class I, with the four-form pattern -o/-a/-i/-e. Mia sorella is f.sg, so the predicative form is alta.

Simpatico ends in -co, which would raise a stem change question in the plural (simpatici? simpatichi?). But in the f.sg the question doesn't arise: the -a ending is simply added. Giulia is f.sg, so simpatica.
examiner_note: Useful to plant the seed that the c-sound choice happens only in the plural, not the singular.

explanation: The full Class I adjective pattern is -o (m.sg), -a (f.sg), -i (m.pl), -e (f.pl). For a f.pl noun like mele, the form is -e. So le mele sono rosse.
Italian adjectives have to match both gender and number with the noun. Rosso is Class I, so the f.pl form is rosse. Le macchine is f.pl: le macchine sono rosse.
The diagnostic miss here is rossi, over-applying the m.pl ending to a feminine plural. Rosso is Class I; the f.pl form is rosse. Le case is f.pl, so le case sono rosse.
examiner_note: The diagnostic miss here is 'rossi' (wrong gender — over-applying m.pl). Listed first in must_not_include for visibility.

explanation: Italian adjectives agree in gender as well as number. Italiano is Class I, so the f.pl form is italiane. The wrong answer italiani is m.pl, which doesn't match a feminine subject.
examiner_note: Italiani is the diagnostic wrong choice (m.pl over-application).

translation.reveal: cugina - cousin (f); cugino is the m

explanation: Possessives like mie also agree, and confirm the gender and number — here f.pl. Carino is Class I, so the f.pl form is carine. Le mie cugine sono molto carine.

Some Italian adjectives have only two forms — singular -e and plural -i — and don't distinguish gender. These are Class II adjectives. Intelligente is Class II; with a m.sg subject like Marco, the form is intelligente. The wrong intelligento would force it into Class I.
examiner_note: Diagnostic miss 'intelligento' is the classic Class I over-application.

explanation: Class II adjectives don't distinguish gender in the singular: just -e for both. Felice is Class II; un fratello is m.sg, so the form is felice. Working: ho un fratello molto felice.
Italian Class II adjectives don't distinguish gender in the singular — the form is -e for both m.sg and f.sg. Verde is Class II. Il prato is m.sg, so il prato è verde.

Class II adjectives have a single singular form ending in -e, used for both genders. Interessante is Class II; il libro is m.sg, so the form stays interessante. The wrong interessanto would force it into Class I.
Italian adjectives ending in -ile (difficile, facile, utile) are all Class II. Difficile is Class II; questo esercizio is m.sg, so the form is difficile. No -o ending here, unlike Class I.

Class II adjectives shift from -e in the singular to -i in the plural, for both genders. Intelligente is Class II; i ragazzi is m.pl, so the form is intelligenti. The classic miss is leaving -e unchanged.
examiner_note: Diagnostic miss is leaving -e (no number agreement).

explanation: Class II adjectives form their plural with -i for both genders. Verde is Class II; gli alberi is m.pl, so the predicative form is verdi.
Class II plural is -i regardless of gender. Felice is Class II; i gemelli is m.pl, so the predicative form is felici.
Class II adjectives shift -e (singular) to -i (plural). Interessante is Class II; i libri is m.pl, so the form is interessanti. The unchanged interessante would miss number agreement.
Class II adjectives ending in -ile (facile, utile, possibile) form their plural with -i: facili, utili, possibili. Questi esercizi is m.pl, so the form is facili.

The headline difference between Class II and Class I: Class II makes no gender distinction in the singular. Intelligente is Class II; la ragazza is f.sg, so the form is still intelligente. The wrong intelligenta would force it into Class I.
examiner_note: Diagnostic miss is 'intelligenta'.

explanation: Class II adjectives don't change for gender in the singular: -e covers both m.sg and f.sg. Verde is Class II; l'erba is f.sg, so the form is still verde.
Class II adjectives keep the -e ending in the singular for both genders. Felice is Class II; una sorella is f.sg, so the attributive form is felice.

prompt: Which is right? 'Maria è una persona ____.' (gentile)

gender.reveal: feminine (f); used for both males and females

explanation: Class II adjectives have a single singular form ending in -e for both genders. Gentile is Class II; una persona is f.sg, so the form is gentile. The wrong gentila would force it into Class I.
Class II adjectives ending in -ile keep -ile for both genders in the singular. Difficile is Class II; questa lingua is f.sg, so the form is difficile.

The tree-noted hardest case for English learners: Class II feminine plural takes -i, not -e. The same -i form covers both m.pl and f.pl in Class II. Intelligente is Class II; le ragazze is f.pl, so intelligenti. Leaving the form unchanged is the classic miss.
examiner_note: Tree-noted as the hardest case for English learners. The classic miss is 'intelligente' (no number agreement) which the must_not_include catches.

explanation: Class II adjectives shift from -e to -i in the plural regardless of gender. Verde is Class II; le piante is f.pl, so the form is verdi.

prompt: Which is right? 'Le mie zie sono molto ____.' (gentile)
explanation: Class II adjectives use -i for both m.pl and f.pl. Gentile is Class II; le zie is f.pl, so the form is gentili. Leaving gentile unchanged is the classic miss for this hardest-case bucket.
examiner_note: Gentile (no change) is the diagnostic miss for this tree-noted hardest-case bucket.

markpoints.label: f.pl with high-frequency Class II adjective

explanation: Grande is Class II — singular -e for both genders, plural -i for both. Le case is f.pl, so le case sono grandi. The Class I confusion 'grande' (no number agreement) is the classic miss.
examiner_note: Tree typo flag: e_class.feminine_plural common_miss reads 'case grande' twice; one of those should be 'case grandi' (Class I confusion).

explanation: Class II adjectives ending in -ile shift to -ili in the plural for both genders. Utile is Class II; queste lezioni is f.pl, so utili.

prompt: Choose the right ending: 'Ho letto due frasi ____.' (importante)
explanation: Class II f.pl is -i, audibly different from the singular -e. Importante is Class II; due frasi is f.pl, so the form is importanti. The Class I miss importanta and the unchanged importante are both wrong.
examiner_note: Extra item on the tree-noted hardest-case bucket; one above the floor of 5.

explanation: When you add an -i ending to an adjective in -co, the c could go soft. Italian inserts an h to keep the hard c-sound, for adjectives with penultimate stress. This is stem change. Bianco has penult stress, so the m.pl is bianchi: dei fiori bianchi.
examiner_note: Bianci (soft c, no h) is the diagnostic miss.

explanation: Penult-stressed -co adjectives take -chi in the m.pl, with the h preserving the hard c-sound. This is stem change. Bianco fits the rule; i muri is m.pl, so bianchi.

prompt: Complete: 'Dopo la maratona i corridori erano ____.' (stanco)
explanation: Stanco follows the same h-insertion rule as bianco: penult-stressed -co adjectives take -chi in the m.pl. I corridori is m.pl, so the predicative form is stanchi.
Penult-stressed -co adjectives keep their hard c in the m.pl by inserting an h: -co → -chi. Bianco fits; i sacchi is m.pl, so bianchi. The wrong bianci would be a soft-c miss.
The h-insertion stem change keeps -co adjectives hard in the m.pl when stress is on the penult. Bianco → bianchi; due divani is m.pl, so divani bianchi.
The same h-insertion rule applies in the feminine: -ca adjectives take -che in the f.pl to keep the hard c-sound. This is stem change. Bianca → bianche; le scarpe is f.pl, so bianche.
For -ca adjectives in the f.pl, Italian inserts an h to preserve the hard c-sound. The f.sg form bianca shifts to f.pl bianche. Le pareti is f.pl, so bianche.
examiner_note: Bianchi here would mean a learner has both got the stem change wrong and missed the gender; bianci shows a soft-c miss; bianca shows no number.

explanation: F.pl of stanca is stanche by the same h-insertion rule that gives bianca → bianche. Le bambine is f.pl, so stanche.
F.pl of bianca is bianche: h-insertion keeps the hard c-sound. Le nuvole is f.pl, so bianche. The wrong bianci would mean soft c plus the wrong gender.
Poco follows the same h-insertion rule as bianco: -ca → -che in the f.pl, -co → -chi in the m.pl. Le tovaglie is f.pl, so poche.
examiner_note: Poco follows the same h-insertion rule. Diagnostic miss is 'poce' (soft c).

explanation: The h-insertion stem change also applies to -go adjectives: m.pl is -ghi, preserving the hard g-sound. Lungo → lunghi; questi racconti is m.pl, so lunghi.
examiner_note: Lungi (soft g) is the diagnostic miss.

translation.reveal: capello - (a single) hair; capelli is hair in general

explanation: Capelli is m.pl (irregular plural of capello, used for hair in general). Lungo takes the h-insertion stem change in the m.pl: lunghi.

prompt: Complete: 'I fiumi del Nord Italia sono ____.' (largo)
explanation: Largo follows the same h-insertion rule as lungo: m.pl is larghi, preserving the hard g-sound. I fiumi is m.pl, so larghi.
M.pl of lungo is lunghi by h-insertion: the h keeps the g hard. I corridoi is m.pl, so lunghi. The wrong lungi would be a soft-g miss.

gender.reveal: masculine plural (m.pl); singular pantalone is rarely used

explanation: Pantaloni is grammatically m.pl (the singular pantalone is rarely used), so an agreeing adjective takes the m.pl form. Lungo takes h-insertion in the m.pl: lunghi.
F.pl of lunga is lunghe by h-insertion: the h preserves the hard g-sound. Le settimane is f.pl, so lunghe.
Lungo follows the h-insertion rule across both genders in the plural: -ghi for m.pl, -ghe for f.pl. Le strade is f.pl, so lunghe.
Largo also takes h-insertion in the f.pl: largo → larga (f.sg) → larghe (f.pl). Le tavole is f.pl, so larghe.

prompt: Which is right? 'Le mie giornate sono molto ____.' (lungo)
explanation: F.pl of lunga is lunghe: h-insertion keeps the g hard. Le giornate is f.pl, so lunghe. The wrong lunge would be a soft-g miss.

gender.reveal: feminine (f); also orecchio (m), with mixed plurals

explanation: Orecchie is f.pl (orecchio also exists as m). Lungo agrees in the attributive position with h-insertion: f.pl is lunghe.

Stem change for -co adjectives depends on stress: penult-stressed words insert h (bianco → bianchi), antepenult-stressed words don't. Simpatico has antepenult stress (sim-pa-ti-co), so m.pl is simpatici with a soft c. The wrong simpatichi over-applies the bianco rule.
examiner_note: Simpatichi is the diagnostic over-application of the bianco-style -chi rule.

gender.reveal: common-gender noun; m.pl colleghi, f.pl colleghe

explanation: Antepenult-stressed -co adjectives don't take h-insertion in the m.pl. Simpatico's stress falls on -pa-, so m.pl is simpatici with soft c. Colleghi is m.pl, so the predicative form is simpatici.
Pratico carries stress on the antepenult (pra-ti-co), so the stress rule for -co gives m.pl pratici with soft c, not pratichi. Problemi is m.pl, so pratici.

prompt: Choose the pair that completes both: 'i muri ____' (bianco) and 'i ragazzi ____' (simpatico)

markpoints.label: stress rule discrimination across two words

explanation: The stress rule for -co adjectives: penult stress takes -chi (bianco → bianchi, hard c preserved), antepenult stress takes -ci (simpatico → simpatici, soft c). Both come up here; the right pair is bianchi & simpatici.
examiner_note: Single markpoint covers the discrimination because the choice is presented as a pair.

explanation: Fantastico stresses the antepenult (fan-tas-ti-co), so the stress rule for -co gives m.pl fantastici with soft c. Questi film is m.pl, so fantastici.
Amico is irregular: it has penultimate stress (a-mi-co) so the rule would predict amichi, but the actual m.pl is amici with soft c. This is a learned exception. Marco e Luigi is m.pl, so amici.
examiner_note: Amichi is the diagnostic over-application of the predictable -chi rule. References proposed bucket stem_changes.irregular.amico_amici.

prompt: Complete: 'Carlo e Marco sono ____ da molti anni.' (amico)
explanation: Amico is one of the few exceptions to the h-insertion rule for penult-stressed -co adjectives. M.pl is the irregular amici with soft c. Carlo e Marco is m.pl, so amici.

prompt: Which is right? 'I miei migliori ____ vivono a Roma.' (amico)
explanation: M.pl of amico is the irregular amici, not amichi: the stress would predict -chi but amico is an exception. The wrong amichi over-applies the regular bianco-style rule.

translation.reveal: amico - friend (m); amica is the f

explanation: The amico irregularity applies only to the m.pl. Feminine plural is regular: amica → amiche with the normal h-insertion. Le mie amiche is f.pl, so amiche.
examiner_note: Useful counterpoint: it tests that the learner does not over-generalise the irregularity.

explanation: I tuoi amici is m.pl; amico is irregular and takes amici in the m.pl, not amichi. The possessive tuoi confirms the m.pl agreement.
Greco is irregular like amico: stress would predict m.pl grechi, but the actual form is greci with soft c. This is a learned exception. Gli amici di Carlo is m.pl, so greci.
examiner_note: Grechi is the diagnostic over-application. References proposed bucket stem_changes.irregular.greco_greci.

prompt: Complete: 'Gli antichi filosofi ____ vivevano ad Atene.' (greco)
explanation: Greco is irregular: m.pl is greci with soft c, not grechi. Filosofi is m.pl, so greci in the attributive position.
Greco's m.pl is the irregular greci, not grechi: an exception to the h-insertion rule for penult-stressed -co adjectives. Templi is m.pl, so greci.
The greco irregularity applies only to the m.pl. Feminine plural is regular: greca → greche with the normal h-insertion. Le isole greche.
examiner_note: Same shape as adj_irr_amico_04: tests no over-generalisation.

explanation: Yogurt is an invariable noun, but the article gli signals m.pl. Greco is irregular: m.pl is greci with soft c, not grechi.

Antico runs the other way to amico: the antepenult stress rule would predict antici (compare simpatici), but antico takes the h-insertion form antichi. This is a counter-irregular. Manoscritti is m.pl, so antichi.
examiner_note: Antici is the diagnostic over-application of the stress rule. References proposed bucket stem_changes.irregular.antico_antichi.

explanation: Antico's m.pl is antichi, not antici: the antepenult stress would predict -ci but antico is an exception. Libri is m.pl, so antichi.
Despite antepenult stress (an-ti-co), antico keeps the -chi plural antichi as an exception to the stress rule. Reperti is m.pl, so antichi. The wrong antici applies the simpatico rule.

prompt: Complete both: 'i miei colleghi ____' (simpatico) and 'i libri ____' (antico)

markpoints.label: stress rule does not fire for antico

explanation: Both have antepenult stress, but only simpatico follows the stress rule for -co adjectives (m.pl simpatici, soft c). Antico is the canonical exception: m.pl antichi, with h-insertion despite the stress. This contrast pair tests both directions.
examiner_note: Two markpoints, two buckets, deliberate contrast. Useful for diagnosing whether the learner is over- or under-applying the stress rule.

prompt: Complete: 'Le ____ tradizioni si conservano nei borghi.' (antico)
explanation: Feminine plural of antico is antiche, the regular -ca → -che h-insertion. The pre-noun position here is slightly literary; tradizioni antiche would also be fine. Tradizioni is f.pl, so antiche.

In Italian, descriptive adjectives — colour, shape, nationality — sit after the noun. This is the unmarked post-noun position. Rossa is a colour, so la casa rossa, not la rossa casa. The fronted version would feel English-style.
examiner_note: La rossa casa is the English-style fronting miss.

prompt: Which is right in Italian? a) la macchina rossa b) la rossa macchina
explanation: Italian colour adjectives go after the noun by default, in the unmarked post-noun position. La macchina rossa is right; la rossa macchina would feel like English-style fronting and is much rarer.

Descriptive adjectives in both classes — including Class II ones like interessante — go after the noun by default. Un libro interessante. The pre-noun version un interessante libro is grammatical but stylistically marked.
examiner_note: Un interessante libro is grammatical but marked; the unmarked default is post-noun.

explanation: Nationality and category adjectives sit firmly after the noun in Italian, even more strongly than descriptive ones. I ragazzi italiani. The pre-noun version is essentially ungrammatical here.
examiner_note: Nationality adjectives have stronger post-noun preference than descriptive ones. Pre-noun is essentially ungrammatical here.

prompt: Which is right? 'Una macchina ____.' (verde — and place it correctly)
explanation: Italian colour adjectives go after the noun in the unmarked post-noun position. Una macchina verde is right; una verde macchina would feel like English-style fronting.

A small set of common adjectives — piccolo, grande, buono, bello, vecchio, nuovo — sit before the noun by default, in canonical pre-noun position. Una piccola casa is the unmarked order. Una casa piccola is also grammatical but marked, emphasising literal size.
examiner_note: If the learner writes 'una casa piccola' they have not made a grammar error, but they have missed the unmarked canonical position. Mark wrong but explain.

explanation: Nuovo belongs to the canonical pre-noun set in its relational sense (a different/another house). Una nuova casa: the speaker has a new one, but it could be any age. Una casa nuova would emphasise 'brand-new' physically, a different reading.
examiner_note: Open uncertainty: 'una casa nuova' is also good Italian. Reviewer to decide whether to relax must_not_include.

prompt: Which is the most natural Italian for 'a young student'? a) un giovane studente b) uno studente giovane
explanation: Giovane belongs to the canonical pre-noun set for typical age/size adjectives. Un giovane studente is the unmarked order. Uno studente giovane is also grammatical but marked.

With abstract nouns like problema, piccolo in pre-noun position gives the figurative reading 'minor, slight'. Un piccolo problema = a minor issue. Un problema piccolo would force a literal 'physically small problem' reading, which makes no sense.
Bravo belongs to the canonical pre-noun set. Un bravo studente is the unmarked order, conveying overall good qualities (skilled, well-behaved). Uno studente bravo is also acceptable but less common.

prompt: Which phrase means 'a long-standing friend' (not a friend who is elderly)?
explanation: Vecchio is one of a small set of Italian adjectives that change meaning by position. Pre-noun it carries the relational reading: long-standing, established. Un vecchio amico = a friend of many years. Post-noun, un amico vecchio, would mean physically old.
examiner_note: Migrated 2026-05-15 from aggregate position.semantic_shift to word-leaf position.semantic_shift.vecchio. Forward-references the deferred bucket; will resolve when the architect promotes the leaf.

prompt: Place the adjective for 'a great man (great in achievements)': un ____ uomo or un uomo ____ ? (grande)

translation.reveal: grande - big, great (pre-noun: great figurative; post-noun: tall/big literal)

explanation: Grande changes meaning by position. Pre-noun it's the figurative reading: great in stature, reputation. Un grande uomo = a great man in achievements. Post-noun, un uomo grande, would mean physically large.
examiner_note: Migrated 2026-05-15 from aggregate position.semantic_shift to word-leaf position.semantic_shift.grande. Forward-references the deferred bucket; will resolve when the architect promotes the leaf.

prompt: Place the adjective for 'a tall man (physically large)': un ____ uomo or un uomo ____ ? (grande)

translation.reveal: grande - big, great (pre-noun: great figurative; post-noun: tall/big literal)

explanation: Grande in post-noun position gives the literal reading: physically big or tall. Un uomo grande = a physically large man. The figurative 'great in achievements' would be the pre-noun un grande uomo.
examiner_note: Migrated 2026-05-15 from aggregate position.semantic_shift to word-leaf position.semantic_shift.grande. Forward-references the deferred bucket; will resolve when the architect promotes the leaf.

prompt: Which means 'a dear (beloved) friend' rather than 'an expensive friend'?
explanation: Caro shifts dramatically between positions. Pre-noun gives 'dear, beloved': un caro amico = a beloved friend. Post-noun gives the literal price reading: un amico caro = an expensive friend, which is concrete and almost humorous in context.
examiner_note: Caro is a strong example because the post-noun reading is concrete and almost humorous in context. Kept at aggregate position.semantic_shift bucket: no word-leaf was proposed for caro in this batch; if the architect wants a position.semantic_shift.caro leaf, this item should be migrated.

prompt: Place the adjective: 'the poor (unfortunate) boy' = il ____ ragazzo or il ragazzo ____ ? (povero)
explanation: Povero shifts meaning by position. Pre-noun is the empathetic reading: 'unfortunate, to be pitied'. Il povero ragazzo = the unfortunate boy. Post-noun, il ragazzo povero, would mean the boy with little money.
examiner_note: Migrated 2026-05-15 from aggregate position.semantic_shift to word-leaf position.semantic_shift.povero. Forward-references the deferred bucket; will resolve when the architect promotes the leaf.

explanation: Adjectives ending in -cio (vecchio, liscio) drop the unstressed -i when the ending becomes -i in the m.pl. This is stem change. Vecchio → vecchi, not vecchii. I miei zii is m.pl, so vecchi.
examiner_note: Vecchii (double i) is the classic over-preservation miss. Vecchichi shows confusion with the bianco-style -chi rule.

prompt: Complete: 'I miei nonni hanno comprato dei mobili ____.' (vecchio)
explanation: When -cio adjectives form the m.pl, the unstressed -i drops to avoid the double-i form vecchii. This is stem change. Mobili is m.pl, so vecchi.

prompt: Which is right? 'I capelli di Marco sono ____.' (liscio)
explanation: Liscio follows the -cio drop-i rule: stem change gives m.pl lisci, not liscii. Capelli is m.pl, so lisci. The wrong liscii preserves the unstressed -i.
examiner_note: Capelli is listed as a separate lemma so the clickable-word match works on the plural surface form.

explanation: M.pl of vecchio drops the unstressed -i: vecchi. Questi divani is m.pl, so the predicative form is vecchi. This is stem change in -cio adjectives.

prompt: Complete: 'Hanno trovato dei vasi ____ in cantina.' (vecchio)
explanation: Vasi is m.pl. Vecchio is a -cio adjective: stem change drops the unstressed -i in the m.pl, giving vecchi.

gender.reveal: masculine plural (m.pl); singular pantalone is rarely used

explanation: Adjectives ending in -gio (grigio, saggio) drop the unstressed -i when forming the m.pl. This is stem change. Grigio → grigi, not grigii. Pantaloni is m.pl, so grigi.
examiner_note: Grigii (double i) is the classic over-preservation miss. Grighi shows confusion with the lungo-style -ghi rule.

explanation: Occhi is m.pl (irregular plural of occhio). Grigio is a -gio adjective: stem change drops the unstressed -i in the m.pl, giving grigi.

prompt: Which is right? 'Sono dei consigli molto ____.' (saggio)
explanation: Saggio follows the -gio drop-i rule: stem change gives m.pl saggi, not saggii. Consigli is m.pl, so the form is saggi.
Guanti is m.pl. Grigio is a -gio adjective: stem change drops the unstressed -i in the m.pl, giving grigi.

prompt: Complete: 'Solo gli uomini ____ sanno tacere al momento giusto.' (saggio)
explanation: Uomini is m.pl (irregular plural of uomo). Saggio is a -gio adjective: stem change drops the unstressed -i, giving saggi.
Adjectives ending in -cia (vecchia, liscia) drop the unstressed -i when forming the f.pl. This is stem change. Vecchia → vecchie (not vecchice). Le mie zie is f.pl, so vecchie.

prompt: Complete: 'Le mie nonne hanno delle sedie ____.' (vecchio)
explanation: Sedie is f.pl. Vecchio f.sg is vecchia, and the f.pl drops the unstressed -i: vecchie. So sedie vecchie.
Liscia f.pl is lisce: the unstressed -i drops as -cia → -ce. This is stem change. Pareti is f.pl, so lisce. The wrong liscie preserves the -i.
examiner_note: Liscie (preserved i) is the diagnostic over-preservation miss.

prompt: Complete: 'Queste foto di famiglia sono molto ____.' (vecchio)

gender.reveal: feminine (f); invariable in form (la foto, le foto)

explanation: Foto is invariable as a noun but takes f.pl agreement here, signalled by the determiner queste. Vecchio is a -cia adjective in the feminine: stem change drops the unstressed -i in the f.pl, giving vecchie.
examiner_note: Tests that the learner agrees with the determiner (queste), not the surface form of the noun.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho ritrovato le mie scarpe ____ in cantina.' (vecchio)
explanation: Scarpe is f.pl. Vecchia f.pl is vecchie: stem change drops the unstressed -i as -cia → -ce.

any_phrases.note: widely accepted modern variant; the bucket description allows both

explanation: Adjectives in -gia (grigia, saggia) traditionally drop the unstressed -i in the f.pl (grige), but modern usage also accepts the -i preserved (grigie). Le scarpe is f.pl, so grige or grigie. Both at full credit.
examiner_note: The bucket description explicitly allows both forms at full credit. Diagnostic misses are 'grigia' (no plural) and 'grigi' (wrong gender).

prompt: Complete: 'Le nuvole sopra il mare erano ____.' (grigio)
explanation: Nuvole is f.pl. Grigia f.pl is grige (with the unstressed -i dropped) or grigie (modern variant). Both are accepted at full credit.

prompt: Which is right? 'Le sue parole erano ____ e ponderate.' (saggio)
explanation: Saggia f.pl is sagge: the unstressed -i drops as -gia → -ge. This is stem change. Parole is f.pl, so sagge. The variant saggie is tolerated but non-standard.
examiner_note: Saggie is sometimes seen but standard usage prefers sagge; flagged as 0.8 credit.

explanation: Rocce is f.pl. Grigia f.pl is grige (dropping the unstressed -i) or grigie (preserving it). Both are accepted.

prompt: Complete: 'Sono state delle decisioni ____ in un momento difficile.' (saggio)
explanation: Decisioni is f.pl. Saggia f.pl is sagge: the unstressed -i drops as -gia → -ge. This is stem change.

prompt: Complete: 'I ragazzi e le ragazze sono ____.' (italiano)
explanation: When an adjective qualifies a mixed-gender plural group, Italian defaults to the masculine plural form. This is mixed-gender plural. I ragazzi e le ragazze together are mixed, so the adjective is italiani — even though the closer noun is feminine.
examiner_note: Italiane (agreeing with the closer feminine noun) is the canonical English-style proximity miss.

prompt: Which is right? 'Mio padre e mia madre sono molto ____.' (alto)
explanation: Mixed-gender plural takes the m.pl form by default in Italian. Father is m and mother is f, so the pair is mixed: the agreeing adjective is alti.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho un fratello e due sorelle. Loro sono molto ____.' (simpatico)
explanation: Even when the feminines outnumber the masculines (one brother, two sisters), mixed-gender plural takes the m.pl form. Simpatico has antepenult stress, so m.pl is simpatici with soft c.
examiner_note: Crosses with the stress-conditioned -ci rule (simpatico is antepenult-stressed); both buckets are relevant. Filed under mixed-gender as the diagnostic novelty.

prompt: Complete: 'I miei zii e le mie zie sono tutti ____.' (felice)
explanation: Mixed-gender plural takes m.pl by default. Felice is Class II: m.pl is felici. The surface form happens to coincide with f.pl, but the mixed-gender rule fires either way.
examiner_note: Class II surface form coincides with f.pl. Bucket fires either way, but the diagnostic is that the learner knows the m.pl rule applies.

prompt: Complete: 'Marco, Luca e Anna sono ____ dopo il viaggio.' (stanco)
explanation: Two males and one female make a mixed-gender plural, so the adjective takes m.pl. Stanco has penult stress, so the m.pl takes h-insertion stem change: stanchi.
examiner_note: Compound bucket touch: also exercises stem_changes.predictable.co_chi. Mixed-gender is the diagnostic novelty.

explanation: Colours borrowed from nouns — rosa (rose), viola (violet), lilla (lilac) — don't inflect. They're invariable adjectives. Rosa stays rosa across all gender/number combinations: le case rosa.
examiner_note: Rose (over-applied Class I plural) is the diagnostic miss.

explanation: Blu is an invariable adjective: the same form covers all gender and number combinations. I pantaloni blu, le case blu.

prompt: Which is right? 'Le pareti della camera sono ____.' (viola)
explanation: Viola, borrowed from the flower name (violet), is an invariable adjective. Le pareti viola — no inflection regardless of the noun.

markpoints.label: rosa stays invariant in f.pl context

explanation: Magliette is f.pl, but the invariable adjective rosa stays the same: magliette rosa. The wrong rose would also look like the noun 'roses', creating real-world ambiguity.
examiner_note: The diagnostic miss 'rose' would also look like a noun (roses), creating a real-world ambiguity the explanation can flag.

explanation: Lilla, borrowed from the flower name, is invariable. The form stays lilla regardless of the noun's gender or number.

prompt: Complete: 'Le pareti della cucina sono ____.' (verde scuro)
explanation: Compound colours — two-word colour expressions like verde scuro, blu marino — are invariable as a unit. Le pareti verde scuro, not verdi scure (full agreement) or verde scure (half agreement, also wrong).
examiner_note: The diagnostic full-agreement miss 'verdi scure' is the classic one. Half-agreement 'verde scure' is also common and worth catching.

prompt: Which is right? 'Le porte sono ____.' (blu marino)
explanation: Compound colours stay invariable as a unit. Blu marino doesn't inflect for the noun's gender or number: le porte blu marino.
Giallo limone is a compound colour: invariable as a unit. Le camicie giallo limone, not gialle limoni.

prompt: Complete: 'Hanno scelto delle tende ____ per il salotto.' (rosso fuoco)
explanation: Rosso fuoco is a compound colour: invariable across all gender and number combinations. Le tende rosso fuoco.
Pari is an invariable adjective: same form for all genders and numbers. I numeri pari = the even numbers.

prompt: Complete: 'Alla fine della partita le squadre erano ____.' (pari)
explanation: Pari in the 'equal, level' sense (tied score) is the same invariable adjective as 'even' (numbers). The form stays pari: le squadre pari.
Dispari ('odd, uneven') is an invariable adjective like pari. The form stays dispari for all genders and numbers.

Pre-noun bello changes form to match what article the noun would take. Like a definite article, it has an allomorph series. Before m.sg nouns taking il (most consonants), bello becomes bel: un bel ragazzo, parallel to il ragazzo.
examiner_note: The pre-noun form parallels the definite article: il -> bel. The classic miss is 'un bello ragazzo' (wrong allomorph).

markpoints.label: bello before m.sg s+consonant or z

explanation: Pre-noun bello parallels the definite article. Before m.sg nouns that take lo (s+consonant, z, gn, ps, x, y), bello keeps its full form: un bello zaino, parallel to lo zaino.
examiner_note: The article parallel here is lo: lo zaino -> bello zaino.

explanation: Pre-noun bello elides before a vowel, like the definite article l'. Before m.sg vowel-initial nouns, the form is bell': un bell'amico, parallel to l'amico. This is elision.
In the feminine singular before a consonant, pre-noun bello takes the regular -a ending: bella. Parallel to la casa, the form is una bella casa.
In the m.pl before a consonant, pre-noun bello becomes bei (parallel to i libri). Dei bei libri. The classic miss belli is the standalone/post-noun plural, not the pre-noun allomorph.
examiner_note: Belli is the standalone/post-noun plural; bei is the pre-noun plural before most consonants.

prompt: Complete: 'Ci sono dei ____ zaini in vetrina.' (bello)
explanation: Before m.pl nouns that take gli (vowel-initial, s+consonant, z), pre-noun bello becomes begli: dei begli zaini, parallel to gli zaini. The classic miss bei would be the consonant-form allomorph.
examiner_note: Bei (the m.pl consonant form) is the diagnostic miss here.

explanation: In the f.pl, pre-noun bello takes the regular -e ending: belle. Parallel to le case, the form is delle belle case.
Pre-noun buono parallels the indefinite article. Before m.sg nouns that take un (consonant or vowel), buono shortens to buon: un buon amico, parallel to un amico.
examiner_note: Buono libro is the classic over-application miss.

markpoints.label: buono before m.sg s+consonant or z

explanation: Before m.sg nouns that take uno (s+consonant, z), pre-noun buono can keep its full form: un buono studente. Many speakers prefer the post-noun position (uno studente bravo) or use bravo, but the allomorph rule licenses the pre-noun buono here.
examiner_note: Open uncertainty: native speakers also frequently say 'un bravo studente' or post-pose buono. The pre-noun shortening question is the focus here.

explanation: Before a m.sg vowel-initial noun, pre-noun buono shortens to buon, with no apostrophe — parallel to un inizio. So un buon inizio.
In the f.sg before a consonant, pre-noun buono takes the regular -a ending: buona. Parallel to una giornata, the form is una buona giornata.
The buon shortening only happens in the singular before a noun. In the plural, buono follows the regular Class I pattern: buoni for m.pl, buone for f.pl. Ragazzi is m.pl, so buoni.
The buon shortening is strictly a pre-noun phenomenon. In predicative position (after 'to be') or post-noun, no shortening happens: il vino è buono, the full Class I form.
examiner_note: Tests that the learner does not over-apply the buon shortening to non-pre-noun positions.

markpoints.label: gran or grande, both accepted in pre-noun singular

explanation: Pre-noun grande in the singular can shorten to gran or keep its full form. Both un gran successo and un grande successo are accepted in Italian. Gran is more common in fixed expressions.
examiner_note: Both forms full credit. The diagnostic miss would be 'grandi' (pluralising in a singular context).

explanation: Before a m.sg noun, pre-noun grande may shorten to gran or stay full: un grande problema and un gran problema are both accepted in modern Italian.

prompt: Complete: 'Hanno dei ____ progetti per il futuro.' (grande)
explanation: The gran shortening only applies in the singular. In the plural, grande is invariably grandi — Class II: -e singular shifts to -i plural for both genders.

prompt: Complete: 'Ha una ____ passione per la musica.' (grande)
explanation: Grande is Class II, so there's no separate feminine form: f.sg is grande for both genders. The gran shortening also works in the f.sg: una grande passione or una gran passione, both accepted.
examiner_note: Granda is the diagnostic over-applied-Class-I miss.

prompt: Complete: 'La chiesa di ____ Marco è famosa.' (santo)

markpoints.label: san before male saint with consonant

explanation: Pre-noun santo before a male saint's name has its own allomorph series. Before consonant-initial names (not s+consonant), santo shortens to san: san Marco. The wrong santo Marco over-applies the full form.
examiner_note: Santo Marco is wrong (over-application of the s+consonant form).

prompt: Complete: 'La festa di ____ Antonio è il 13 giugno.' (santo)

markpoints.label: sant' before male saint with vowel

explanation: Pre-noun santo elides before a vowel-initial male saint's name: sant'Antonio. This is elision, like il → l' in the article.

markpoints.label: santo before male saint with s+consonant

explanation: Before a male saint's name beginning with s+consonant (Stefano, Sebastiano) or z, santo keeps its full form: santo Stefano. Parallels lo Stefano if you used an article.
examiner_note: San Stefano is the diagnostic over-shortening miss.

prompt: Complete: 'La cattedrale di ____ Maria del Fiore.' (santo)

markpoints.label: santa before female saint with consonant

explanation: Female saints take the feminine form santa before a consonant: Santa Maria del Fiore. The feminine of santo follows the regular -o/-a Class I pattern.

prompt: Complete: 'La chiesa di ____ Anna è qui vicino.' (santo)

markpoints.label: sant' before female saint with vowel

explanation: Before a vowel-initial female saint's name, santa elides to sant': sant'Anna. The elided form is identical to the male sant' (e.g. sant'Antonio).
When mezzo functions as an adjective qualifying a noun, it agrees in gender and number. Ora is f.sg, so the form is mezza: tra mezza ora = in half an hour.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho mangiato due ____ pere a colazione.' (mezzo)
explanation: Adjectival mezzo agrees in number as well as gender. Pere is f.pl, so the form is mezze: due mezze pere.
In telling the time, the f.sg mezza is used: it implicitly agrees with the unspoken ora. Sono le sei e mezza. Standard usage prefers mezza; le sei e mezzo is heard colloquially.
examiner_note: 'Sono le sei e mezzo' is also heard colloquially but the standard is mezza.

explanation: Adjectival mezzo agrees with the noun it qualifies. Libro is m.sg, so the form is mezzo: ho letto solo mezzo libro.
Pre-noun quello, like bello, has an allomorph series that parallels the definite article. Before m.sg nouns taking il, the form is quel: quel libro, parallel to il libro.
examiner_note: Like bello, quello inflects to match the article that would precede the noun.

markpoints.label: quello before m.sg s+consonant or z

explanation: Pre-noun quello keeps its full form before m.sg nouns that take lo (s+consonant, z): quello zaino, parallel to lo zaino.
Before m.sg vowel-initial nouns, pre-noun quello elides to quell': quell'amico, parallel to l'amico. This is elision.

prompt: Complete: '____ casa appartiene a mia zia.' (quello)
explanation: In the f.sg before a consonant, pre-noun quello takes the regular -a ending: quella. Parallel to la casa, the form is quella casa.

prompt: Complete: '____ libri sono di mio fratello.' (quello)
explanation: Pre-noun quello pluralises to quei before m.pl consonants: quei libri, parallel to i libri. The classic miss is quelli — that's the standalone pronoun form, not the pre-noun allomorph.
examiner_note: Quelli is the standalone/pronominal form (when quello stands alone for 'those ones'); quei is the attributive pre-noun form.

explanation: Before m.pl nouns that take gli (vowel, s+consonant, z), pre-noun quello becomes quegli: quegli zaini, parallel to gli zaini.
In the f.pl, pre-noun quello takes the regular -e ending: quelle. Parallel to le case, the form is quelle case.
Both adjectives agree with libri (m.pl). Nuovo is Class I, so m.pl is nuovi. Interessante is Class II, so m.pl is interessanti. Different classes, same number rule in the masculine plural.
examiner_note: Two markpoints: fires both class buckets. Useful aggregate item testing that the learner applies each class's plural rule correctly.

explanation: Le ragazze is f.pl. Class I alto takes -e: alte. Class II intelligente takes -i: intelligenti. The two classes diverge here — Class I gets -e in f.pl, Class II gets -i.
examiner_note: Contrasts Class I (-e in f.pl) vs Class II (-i in f.pl). The classic miss for Class II f.pl is leaving 'intelligente' (no change).

prompt: Complete: 'I tuoi colleghi e le tue colleghe sono ____.' (stanco)
explanation: Two rules combine here: mixed-gender plural defaults to m.pl, and stanco's -co stem takes h-insertion in the m.pl. Both give the same surface form: stanchi.
examiner_note: Same answer surface form 'stanchi' fires two distinct buckets. Useful for diagnosing whether the learner has both rules or just one.

prompt: Complete: 'Roma è una città ____ e ____.' (grande, antico)

markpoints.label: Class I f.sg takes -a (no stem-change in singular)

gender.reveal: feminine (f); invariable noun (la città / le città)

explanation: Roma is f.sg. Grande is Class II: no gender distinction in singular, stays grande. Antico is Class I: f.sg takes -a, giving antica. The h-insertion stem change only fires in the plural, not the singular.
examiner_note: Useful for showing that stem-change rules are plural-only. A learner writing 'antiche' here has over-applied the stem-change to a singular context.

prompt: Complete: 'I muri della cucina sono ____ e ____.' (bianco, lungo)
explanation: Both bianco (-co) and lungo (-go) trigger h-insertion in the m.pl: bianchi and lunghi. Same underlying logic — preserve the hard consonant — applied across two predictable stem-change buckets.
examiner_note: Aggregate item that fires both stem-change buckets simultaneously, useful for diagnosing whether the rule generalises (co_chi + go_ghi share the underlying logic of preserving the hard sound).

prompt: Complete: 'Mi piacciono molto le tazze ____ in vetrina.' (verde acqua)

translation.reveal: verde acqua - aqua green, water green

explanation: Verde acqua is a compound colour: invariable as a unit. Le tazze verde acqua stays unchanged regardless of the noun's gender or number.

prompt: Complete: 'L'azienda promuove ____ opportunità per tutti i dipendenti.' (pari)
explanation: 'Pari opportunità' (equal opportunities) is a standard fixed phrase. Pari is invariable as always; opportunità is also an invariable noun, so nothing changes across number.

prompt: Complete: 'È stata una lotta ____ tra le due squadre, una contro un avversario molto più forte.' (impari)
explanation: Impari ('unequal, uneven') is an invariable adjective like pari and dispari. The context 'molto più forte' (much stronger) forces the 'unequal struggle' reading.

prompt: Which means 'Marco is my long-standing colleague (we have worked together for years)'?
choices: Marco è un mio vecchio collega.
Marco è un mio collega vecchio.

markpoints.label: vecchio pre-noun = long-standing
any_phrases: Marco è un mio vecchio collega
must_not_include: Marco è un mio collega vecchio

explanation: Vecchio in pre-noun position carries the relational reading: long-standing, of many years. Marco è un mio vecchio collega = he and the speaker have worked together for years, regardless of his age.
examiner_note: References proposed bucket position.semantic_shift.vecchio (deferred by the architect). Forward-reference is acceptable per brief §2.

prompt: Which means 'Marco is an elderly colleague (he is physically advanced in age)'?
choices: Marco è un mio vecchio collega.
Marco è un mio collega vecchio.

markpoints.label: vecchio post-noun = elderly
any_phrases: Marco è un mio collega vecchio
must_not_include: Marco è un mio vecchio collega

explanation: Vecchio in post-noun position gives the literal reading: physically elderly. Marco è un mio collega vecchio = he is a colleague advanced in age.
examiner_note: Companion to vecchio_01; same materials, opposite meaning.

prompt: Complete: 'Sergio è il mio ____ amico: ci conosciamo dal liceo.' (vecchio)
explanation: The clause 'ci conosciamo dal liceo' (we've known each other since high school) signals the long-standing-friendship reading. Vecchio in pre-noun position carries that relational sense, regardless of age.

prompt: Which means 'Dante was a great writer (great in stature and achievements)'?
choices: Dante è stato un grande scrittore.
Dante è stato uno scrittore grande.

markpoints.label: grande pre-noun = great (figurative)
any_phrases: Dante è stato un grande scrittore
must_not_include: Dante è stato uno scrittore grande

translation.reveal: grande - big, great (pre-noun: great figurative; post-noun: big literal)

explanation: Grande in pre-noun position carries the figurative reading: great in stature, importance, achievement. Dante è stato un grande scrittore — recognised for his literary greatness, not his physical size.
examiner_note: References proposed bucket position.semantic_shift.grande (deferred).

prompt: Which means 'My uncle is a big/tall man (physically large)'?
choices: Mio zio è un grande uomo.
Mio zio è un uomo grande.

markpoints.label: grande post-noun = tall/big (literal)
any_phrases: Mio zio è un uomo grande
must_not_include: Mio zio è un grande uomo

translation.reveal: grande - big, great (pre-noun: great figurative; post-noun: big literal)

explanation: Grande in post-noun position gives the literal reading: physically big or tall. Mio zio è un uomo grande — describing his size, not his importance.

prompt: Complete: 'Leonardo era un ____ pittore del Rinascimento, ricordato da tutti.' (grande)
explanation: Speaking of a famous historical figure ('ricordato da tutti') selects the figurative pre-noun reading: grande = great in stature. Both grande and the shortened gran are acceptable; gran is the allomorph used before some consonants.

prompt: Which means 'The poor (unfortunate) girl has lost her way'?
choices: La povera ragazza si è persa.
La ragazza povera si è persa.

markpoints.label: povero pre-noun = unfortunate (relational)
any_phrases: La povera ragazza si è persa
must_not_include: La ragazza povera si è persa

explanation: Povero in pre-noun position carries the empathetic reading: unfortunate, to be pitied. La povera ragazza si è persa — sympathy with her predicament, not a statement about her finances.
examiner_note: References proposed bucket position.semantic_shift.povero (deferred).

prompt: Which means 'A girl with little money lives next door'?
explanation: Povero in post-noun position carries the literal reading: financially poor. Una ragazza povera vive accanto — describing her economic situation.

prompt: Complete: 'Il ____ uomo non sapeva dove andare. Era una situazione triste e disorientante.' (povero)
explanation: The clause 'era una situazione triste e disorientante' (a sad and disorienting situation) selects the empathetic reading: povero in pre-noun position means 'unfortunate', not 'penniless'.

prompt: Complete: 'Hai un ____ cuore, sei sempre disponibile per gli altri.' (grande)

markpoints.label: gran or grande in pre-noun m.sg (figurative)

explanation: Avere un gran cuore is a fixed expression meaning 'to be big-hearted, generous'. Pre-noun grande in this figurative sense allows both gran and grande, both at full credit.
examiner_note: Fixed expression 'avere un gran cuore' is common in spoken Italian.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho mangiato solo due ____ panini a pranzo.' (mezzo)
explanation: Adjectival mezzo agrees in gender and number. Panini is m.pl, so the form is mezzi: ho mangiato solo due mezzi panini.
examiner_note: Completes the four-form coverage of mezzo across the bucket: mezza (f.sg, item _01), mezze (f.pl, _02), mezza in time (_03), mezzo (m.sg, _04), mezzi (m.pl, _05).
